arXiv:2002.04745v2  [cs.LG]  29 Jun 2020

On Layer Normalization in the Transformer Architecture

Ruibin Xiong † * 1 2 Yunchang Yang * 3 Di He 4 5 Kai Zheng 4 Shuxin Zheng 5 Chen Xing 6 Huishuai Zhang 5 Yanyan Lan 1 2 Liwei Wang 4 3 Tie-Yan Liu

376269f7-52c3-47f0-b6be-381008fad992.label: text_chunk
376269f7-52c3-47f0-b6be-381008fad992.text:
5

Abstract

The Transformer is widely used in natural lan- guage processing tasks. To train a Transformer however, one usually needs a carefully designed learning rate warm-up stage, which is shown to be crucial to the final performance but will slow down the optimization and bring more hyper- parameter tunings. In this paper, we first study theoretically why the learning rate warm-up stage is essential and show that the location of layer nor- malization matters.

f508773f-8d66-43ea-bdf2-e33163ba9630.label: text_chunk
f508773f-8d66-43ea-bdf2-e33163ba9630.text: Specifically, we prove with mean field theory that at initialization, for the original-designed Post-LN Transformer, which places the layer normalization between the resid- ual blocks, the expected gradients of the parame- ters near the output layer are large. Therefore, us- ing a large learning rate on those gradients makes the training unstable. The warm-up stage is prac- tically helpful for avoiding this problem.

2f9ae3bf-c475-49fa-b4b7-bac22f68ae89.label: text_chunk
2f9ae3bf-c475-49fa-b4b7-bac22f68ae89.text: On the other hand, our theory also shows that if the layer normalization is put inside the residual blocks (recently proposed as Pre-LN Transformer), the gradients are well-behaved at initialization. This motivates us to remove the warm-up stage for the training of Pre-LN Transformers. We show in our experiments that Pre-LN Transformers without the warm-up stage can reach comparable results with baselines while requiring significantly less training time and hyper-parameter tuning on a wide range of applications.

d2ed35ac-f7f9-4f04-8969-99ffd1cdd3e2.label: text_chunk
d2ed35ac-f7f9-4f04-8969-99ffd1cdd3e2.text:
1. Introduction

The Transformer ( Vaswani et al. , 2017 ) is one of the most commonly used neural network architectures in natural lan- guage processing. Layer normalization ( Lei Ba et al. , 2016 ) plays a key role in Transformer’s success.

c2d8012e-a2bb-4388-9441-1a3b4f257dda.label: text_chunk
c2d8012e-a2bb-4388-9441-1a3b4f257dda.text:
The originally de- signed Transformer places the layer normalization between the residual blocks, which is usually referred to as the Trans- former with Post-Layer Normalization (Post-LN) ( Wang et al. , 2019 ).

 This architecture has achieved state-of-the-art performance in many tasks including language modeling ( Dai et al. , 2019 ; Al-Rfou et al.

c1013fa6-3d22-4c97-b961-074b2ba0c2ac.label: text_chunk
c1013fa6-3d22-4c97-b961-074b2ba0c2ac.text:
, 2018 ) and machine transla- tion ( Dehghani et al. , 2018 ; Edunov et al. , 2018 ). Unsuper- vised pre-trained models based on the Post-LN Transformer architecture also show impressive performance in many downstream tasks ( Radford et al.

 , 2019 ; Devlin et al. , 2018 ; Yang et al.

254ee0e4-d9ff-4896-b905-405bf4834037.label: text_chunk
254ee0e4-d9ff-4896-b905-405bf4834037.text:
, 2019b ).Despite its great success, people usually need to deal with the optimization of the Post-LN Transformer more carefully than convolutional networks or other sequence-to-sequence models ( Popel & Bojar , 2018 ). In particular, to train the model from scratch, any gradient-based optimization ap- proach requires a learning rate warm-up stage ( Vaswani et al.

 , 2017 ; Liu et al.

3cb0663c-5e9e-45da-b060-8b437b90fc83.label: text_chunk
3cb0663c-5e9e-45da-b060-8b437b90fc83.text:
, 2019a ): the optimization starts with an extremely small learning rate, and then gradually in- creases it to a pre-defined maximum value in a pre-defined number of iterations. Such a warm-up stage not only slows down the optimization process but also brings more hyper- parameter tunings.

 Popel & Bojar ( 2018 ) has shown that the final model performance is quite sensitive to the value of the maximum learning rate and the number of warm-up iterations.

f39b2a51-33a7-456d-8ca4-d0dd6947768d.label: text_chunk
f39b2a51-33a7-456d-8ca4-d0dd6947768d.text:
Tuning such sensitive hyper-parameters is costly in training large-scale models, e.g., BERT ( Devlin et al. , 2018 ) or XLNet ( Yang et al. , 2019b ).

In this paper, we try to alleviate this problem by finding ways to safely remove the learning rate warm-up stage. As the warm-up stage happens in the first several iterations, we investigate the optimization behavior at initialization using mean field theory ( Lee et al.

837f1177-a115-47cc-97b6-37c4fdcf5182.label: text_chunk
837f1177-a115-47cc-97b6-37c4fdcf5182.text:
, 2017 ; Xiao et al. , 2018 ; Yang et al. , 2019a ; Yang , 2019 ; Lee et al. , 2019 ; Zhang et al. , 2019 ).

 According to our theoretical analysis, when putting the layer normalization between the residual blocks, the expected gradients of the parameters near the output layer

371c6404-b44f-461c-ab0e-dcacc5b76d72.label: text_chunk
371c6404-b44f-461c-ab0e-dcacc5b76d72.text: * Equal contribution † Works done while interning at Microsoft Research Asia 1 CAS Key Laboratory of Network Data Science and Technology, Institute of Computing Technolog, Chinese Academy of Sciences 2 University of Chinese Academy of Sci- ences 3 Center for Data Science, Peking University, Beijing Insti- tute of Big Data Research 4 Key Laboratory of Machine Perception, MOE, School of EECS, Peking University 5 Microsoft Research 6 College of Computer Science,

5f999f1d-bc8b-47f7-a44e-8c7e0cdb7e6b.label: text_chunk
5f999f1d-bc8b-47f7-a44e-8c7e0cdb7e6b.text:
Nankai University.

 Correspon- dence to: Shuxin Zheng <shuxin.zheng@microsoft.com>, Di He <dihe@microsoft.com>.Proceedings of the 37 th International Conference on Machine Learning , Vienna, Austria, PMLR 108, 2020. Copyright 2020 by the author(s).On Layer Normalization in the Transformer Architecture
Figure 1.

8951ae28-fe75-463b-b859-e48fd8cedbaa.label: text_chunk
8951ae28-fe75-463b-b859-e48fd8cedbaa.text: (a) Post-LN Transformer layer; (b) Pre-LN Transformer layer.are large. Therefore, without the warm-up stage, directly using a large learning rate to those parameters can make the optimization process unstable. Using a warm-up stage and training the model with small learning rates practically avoid this problem. Extensive experiments are provided to support our theoretical findings.Our theory also shows that the layer normalization plays a crucial role in controlling the gradient scales.

53d3c691-ac5d-453b-a89f-85084e1f997d.label: text_chunk
53d3c691-ac5d-453b-a89f-85084e1f997d.text: This motivates us to investigate whether there are some other ways of po- sitioning the layer normalization that lead to well-behaved gradients. In particular, we study another variant, the Trans- former with Pre-Layer Normalization (Pre-LN) ( Baevski & Auli , 2018 ; Child et al. , 2019 ; Wang et al. , 2019 ).

c2633b7a-42bc-49da-a6ad-adbcc2bc4834.label: text_chunk
c2633b7a-42bc-49da-a6ad-adbcc2bc4834.text: The Pre-LN Transformer puts the layer normalization inside the residual connection and equips with an additional final-layer normalization before prediction (Please see Figure 1 for the differences between the two variants of the Transformer architectures). We show that at initialization, the gradients are well-behaved without any exploding or vanishing for the Pre-LN Transformer both theoretically and empirically.

e9c958e2-a487-494b-a707-e919eeb40384.label: text_chunk
e9c958e2-a487-494b-a707-e919eeb40384.text: Given the gradients are well-behaved in the Pre-LN Trans- former, it is natural to consider removing the learning rate warm-up stage during training. We conduct a variety of experiments, including IWSLT14 German-English transla- tion, WMT14 English-German translation, and BERT pre- training tasks.

1212dd64-baf3-445e-bff3-b2c282d19c10.label: text_chunk
1212dd64-baf3-445e-bff3-b2c282d19c10.text:
We show that, in all tasks, the learning rate warm-up stage can be safely removed, and thus, the number of hyper-parameter is reduced.

 Furthermore, we observe that the loss decays faster for the Pre-LN Transformer model. It can achieve comparable final performances but use much less training time.

b837f481-6659-45dc-bac5-49a03c458685.label: text_chunk
b837f481-6659-45dc-bac5-49a03c458685.text:
This is particularly important for training large-scale models on large-scale datasets.Our contributions are summarized as follows:

• We investigate two Transformer variants, the Post-LN

Transformer and the Pre-LN Transformer, using mean field theory. By studying the gradients at initialization, we pro- vide evidence to show why the learning rate warm-up stage is essential in training the Post-LN Transformer.

ae3a5090-da1c-45ec-9aa5-735976878862.label: text_chunk
ae3a5090-da1c-45ec-9aa5-735976878862.text:
• We are the first to show that the learning-rate warm-up stage can be removed for the Pre-LN Transformer,

 which eases the hyperparameter tuning. We further show that by using proper learning rate schedulers, the training time can be largely reduced on a wide range of applications.

2. Related work

Gradient descent-based methods ( Kingma & Ba , 2014 ; Zeiler , 2012 ; Duchi et al.

020459a3-7a3a-4e1f-8fad-ea7d59dec69e.label: text_chunk
020459a3-7a3a-4e1f-8fad-ea7d59dec69e.text:
, 2011 ; Tieleman & Hinton , 2012 ) are popularly used in optimizing deep neural networks. For convolutional neural networks and recurrent neural net- works, a relatively large learning rate is usually set in the be- ginning, and then decreased along with the optimization pro- cess ( He et al.

 , 2016 ; 2017 ; Sutskever et al. , 2014 ; Gehring et al.

50daee28-dfe1-406a-b16c-37e85feb427e.label: text_chunk
50daee28-dfe1-406a-b16c-37e85feb427e.text: , 2017 ; He et al. , 2019 ). The learning rate warm-up stage has only been shown essential in dealing with some very specific problems, e.g., the large-batch training. Goyal et al. ( 2017 ); He et al. ( 2019 ); You et al.

31f7b817-05c9-4357-b7f8-2f87bd725c8a.label: text_chunk
31f7b817-05c9-4357-b7f8-2f87bd725c8a.text: ( 2018 ) showed that a learning rate warm-up stage is preferred when training neural networks with extremely large batch sizes.However, the learning rate warm-up stage is essential and critical when optimizing the Transformer models in a ma- jority of scenarios ( Vaswani et al. , 2017 ; Devlin et al. , 2018 ; Dai et al. , 2019 ; Radford et al.

748d59f3-82b1-43c2-a1d5-15a6ad34a7a9.label: text_chunk
748d59f3-82b1-43c2-a1d5-15a6ad34a7a9.text:
, 2019 ; Lu et al.

 , 2019 ). Popel & Bojar ( 2018 ) investigated the influence of different warm- up strategies for the optimization of the Post-LN Trans- former model and found that without or with relatively less warm-up iterations, the optimization diverges. The Pre- LN Transformer has been proposed in several recent works ( Baevski & Auli , 2018 ; Child et al.

0836d8d9-99ea-4440-b545-07e2e426e34f.label: text_chunk
0836d8d9-99ea-4440-b545-07e2e426e34f.text:
, 2019 ; Wang et al.

 , 2019 ) to alleviate some optimization issues when training deeper models, but the troublesome warm-up stage still remains in their training pipelines.( Liu et al. , 2019a ) claimed that the benefit of the warm-up stage comes from reducing the variance for the adaptive learning rate in the Adam optimizer ( Kingma & Ba , 2014 ).

b40d7e52-3b1e-4581-85d0-3e9c0f4fb02e.label: text_chunk
b40d7e52-3b1e-4581-85d0-3e9c0f4fb02e.text:
They proposed to rectify the variance of adaptive learning rate by a new variant of Adam called RAdam.

 However, we find that not only for Adam, the learning rate warm-up stage also helps quite a lot for other optimizers. This may indicate that Adam is not the prerequisite for the necessity of the warm-up stage.

a1d34d7e-b996-4d36-b117-7ea43383225e.label: text_chunk
a1d34d7e-b996-4d36-b117-7ea43383225e.text:
In a concurrent and independent work, Nguyen & Salazar ( 2019 ) also empirically observed that the Pre-LN Transformer can be trained without learning rate warm-up stage. Our work provides a more comprehensive study regrading this with a theoretical analysis.

On Layer Normalization in the Transformer Architecture

3. Optimization for the Transformer

3.1. Transformer with Post-Layer Normalization

The Transformer architecture usually consists of stacked Transformer layers ( Vaswani et al.

b7b35d21-7081-443b-bdc1-9d55c58f4453.label: text_chunk
b7b35d21-7081-443b-bdc1-9d55c58f4453.text:
, 2017 ; Devlin et al. , 2018 ), each of which takes a sequence of vectors as input and outputs a new sequence of vectors with the same shape. A Transformer layer has two sub-layers: the (multi-head) self-attention sub-layer and the position-wise feed-forward network sub-layer. Residual connection ( He et al.

 , 2016 ) and layer normalization ( Lei Ba et al.

78e37e4b-ee3c-4500-a94b-6c07c778bc59.label: text_chunk
78e37e4b-ee3c-4500-a94b-6c07c778bc59.text: , 2016 ) are applied for both sub-layers individually. We first introduce each component of the Transformer layer and then present the entire architecture.Self-attention sub-layer An attention function can be formulated as querying an entry with key-value pairs ( Vaswani et al. , 2017 ).

b830e8cd-c6d9-4e20-aad8-794bec456de2.label: text_chunk
b830e8cd-c6d9-4e20-aad8-794bec456de2.text: The self-attention sub-layer uses scaled dot-product attention, which is defined as: Attention ( Q, K, V ) = softmax ( QK T √ d ) V , where d is the di- mensionality of the hidden representations, and Q (Query), K (Key), V (Value) are specified as the hidden represen- tations of the previous layer.

f0482a74-d88a-4c8b-807f-248cd0925f7a.label: text_chunk
f0482a74-d88a-4c8b-807f-248cd0925f7a.text:
The multi-head variant of the self-attention sub-layer is popularly used which allows the model to jointly attend to information from different representation sub-spaces, and is defined as
Multi-head ( Q, K, V ) = Concat ( head 1 , · · · , head H ) W O head k = Attention ( QW Q k , KW K k , V W V k )
,

d44b9653-07e0-4ae1-bbb1-1a9d02efabdd.label: text_chunk
d44b9653-07e0-4ae1-bbb1-1a9d02efabdd.text:
where W Q k ∈ R d × d K , W K k ∈ R d × d K , W V k ∈ R d × d V , and W O ∈ R Hd V × d are project param- eter matrices, H is the number of heads. d K and d V are the dimensionalities of Key and Value. Without any confusion, given a sequence of vectors ( x 1 , ...

, x n ) ,

6ae9ff60-51c7-47c8-9722-aef37f5c3efd.label: text_chunk
6ae9ff60-51c7-47c8-9722-aef37f5c3efd.text: we use MultiHeadAtt ( x i , [ x 1 , x 2 , · · · , x n ]) as the multi-head self-attention mechanism on position i which considers the attention from x i to the en- tire sequence, i.e., MultiHeadAtt ( x i , [ x 1 , x 2 , · · · , x n ]) = Multi-head ( x i , [ x 1 , . . .

c4c43ffe-90a1-450c-bc53-73e4e9144dc3.label: text_chunk
c4c43ffe-90a1-450c-bc53-73e4e9144dc3.text:
, x n ] , [ x 1 , . . . , x n ]) .Position-wise FFN sub-layer In addition to the self- attention sub-layer, each Transformer layer contains a fully connected network, which is applied to each position sep- arately and identically. This sub-layer is a two-layer feed- forward network with a ReLU activation function. Given a sequence of vectors h 1 , ...

, h n ,

558ca115-4e92-4a20-b48b-1b0d15ba7b32.label: text_chunk
558ca115-4e92-4a20-b48b-1b0d15ba7b32.text:
the computation of a position-wise FFN sub-layer on any h i is defined as:
FFN ( h i ) = ReLU ( h i W 1 + b 1 ) W 2 + b 2 , where W 1 , W 2 , b 1 and b 2 are parameters.Residual connection and layer normalization Besides the two sub-layers described above, the residual connection
and layer normalization are also key components to the Transformer.

010af9ed-01ed-4b24-af2b-17a2bb05634f.label: text_chunk
010af9ed-01ed-4b24-af2b-17a2bb05634f.text: For any vector v , the layer normalization is computed as LayerNorm ( v ) = γ v − µ σ + β , in which µ, σ are the mean and standard deviation of the elements in v , i.e., µ = 1 d P d k =1 v k and σ 2 = 1 d P d k =1 ( v k − µ ) 2 . Scale γ and bias vector β are parameters.

c2e2d62b-718d-4195-ad1e-eb22bf9bc04c.label: text_chunk
c2e2d62b-718d-4195-ad1e-eb22bf9bc04c.text:
Different orders of the sub-layers, residual connection and layer normalization in a Transformer layer lead to variants of Transformer architectures. One of the original and most popularly used architecture for the Transformer and BERT ( Vaswani et al. , 2017 ; Devlin et al.

 , 2018 ) follows “self- attention (FFN) sub-layer → residual connection → layer normalization”, which we call the Transformer with Post- Layer normalization (Post-LN Transformer),

cffd0377-3fb7-413b-9528-be59df6176d4.label: text_chunk
cffd0377-3fb7-413b-9528-be59df6176d4.text:
as illustrated in Figure 1 .Post-LN Transformer Denote x l,i as the input of the l -th Transformer layer at position i , where x l,i is a real-valued vector of dimension d , i = 1 , 2 , ...

, n , l = 1 , 2 , ..., L . n is the length of the sequence and L is the number of layers.

175d4450-f251-45d2-8ff6-ffb0f8dc8e64.label: text_chunk
175d4450-f251-45d2-8ff6-ffb0f8dc8e64.text: For completeness, we define x 0 ,i as the input embedding at position i which is usually a combination of word embed- ding and positional embedding.

29a6bbc2-e667-4694-ab9b-3b85811e3ab2.label: text_chunk
29a6bbc2-e667-4694-ab9b-3b85811e3ab2.text:
The computations inside the l -th layer are composed of several steps, and we use super-scripts on x to present the input(output) of different steps as in Table 1 (left), where W 1 ,l , W 2 ,l , b 1 ,l and b 2 ,l are parameters of the FFN sub-layer in the l -th layer.

3.2.

db295004-8914-416c-a8d7-d5b72e8a1fd3.label: text_chunk
db295004-8914-416c-a8d7-d5b72e8a1fd3.text:
The learning rate warm-up stage

We are interested in the learning rate warm-up stage in the optimization of the Post-LN Transformer. Different from the optimization of many other architectures in which the learn- ing rate starts from a relatively large value and then decays ( Bahdanau et al. , 2017 ; Dauphin et al.

76edb159-63b9-4ecf-96cd-a04e9e210bbb.label: text_chunk
76edb159-63b9-4ecf-96cd-a04e9e210bbb.text:
, 2017 ), a learning rate warm-up stage for the Post-LN Transformer seems critical ( Popel & Bojar , 2018 ).

 We denote the learning rate of the t -th iteration as lr ( t ) and the maximum learning rate during training as lr max . Given a predefined time frame T warmup , the learning rate scheduler for the first T warmup iterations ( Vaswani et al.

e330a6f4-7595-44cc-9a18-3777a6cad3f7.label: text_chunk
e330a6f4-7595-44cc-9a18-3777a6cad3f7.text:
, 2018 ) is defined as
lr ( t ) = t T warmup lr max , t ≤ T warmup . (1)


After this warm-up stage, the learning rate will be set by classical learning rate schedulers, such as the linear decay, the inverse square-root decay, or forced decay at particular iterations.

8caf38c8-d5ba-45f2-823d-04b9af1da56e.label: text_chunk
8caf38c8-d5ba-45f2-823d-04b9af1da56e.text:
We conduct experiments to show that this learn- ing rate warm-up stage is essential for training Post-LN Transformer models.Experimental setting We conduct experiments on the IWSLT14 German-to-English (De-En) machine translation
On Layer Normalization in the Transformer Architecture
Figure 2.

 Performances of the models optimized by Adam and SGD on the IWSLT14 De-En task.task.

3208bae4-ec2c-4605-9d4b-e3ef73903d19.label: text_chunk
3208bae4-ec2c-4605-9d4b-e3ef73903d19.text:
We mainly investigate two aspects: whether the learn- ing rate warm-up stage is essential and whether the final model performance is sensitive to the value of T warmup . To study the first aspect, we train the model with the Adam optimizer ( Kingma & Ba , 2014 ) and the vanilla SGD op- timizer ( Ruder , 2016 ) respectively.

 For both optimziers, we check whether the warm-up stage can be removed.

bcd0625e-e860-476c-99d9-8e3a726b3701.label: text_chunk
bcd0625e-e860-476c-99d9-8e3a726b3701.text:
We follow Vaswani et al. ( 2017 ) to set hyper-parameter β to be (0 . 9 , 0 . 98) in Adam. We also test different lr max for both optimizers.

 For Adam, we set lr max = 5 e − 4 or 1 e − 3 , and for SGD, we set lr max = 5 e − 3 or 1 e − 3 .

ca9b0558-8357-4e72-8ebd-125c3baaba27.label: text_chunk
ca9b0558-8357-4e72-8ebd-125c3baaba27.text:
When the warm-up stage is used, we set T warmup = 4000 as suggested by the original paper ( Vaswani et al. , 2017 ).

 To study the second aspect, we set T warmup to be 1/500/4000 (“1” refers to the no warm-up setting) and use lr max = 5 e − 4 or 1 e − 3 with Adam.

d3cde40d-e41a-402d-90ab-985c5564dc56.label: text_chunk
d3cde40d-e41a-402d-90ab-985c5564dc56.text:
For all experiments, a same inverse square root learning rate scheduler is used after the warm-up stage. We use both validation loss and BLEU ( Papineni et al.

 , 2002 ) as the evaluation measure of the model performance.Results and discussions We record the model check- points for every epoch during training and calculate the validation loss and BLEU score. The performance of the models are plotted in Figure 2(a) and Figure 2(b) .

b5e20ce9-e045-427d-a0f5-7cf3f1a78a06.label: text_chunk
b5e20ce9-e045-427d-a0f5-7cf3f1a78a06.text:
The x-axis is the epoch number and the y-axis is the BLEU score/validation loss. "w/o warm-up" indicates “without


the warm-up stage” while "w/ warm-up" indicates “with the warm-up stage”.First, we can see that for both optimizers, the learning rate warm-up stage is essential. Without the warm-up stage, the BLEU score of the model trained with Adam optimizer can only achieve 8.45.

9d10199a-b734-4745-a644-6efa029b9430.label: text_chunk
9d10199a-b734-4745-a644-6efa029b9430.text:
As a comparison, the model trained using the warm-up stage can achieve around 34 in terms of BLEU score.

 The same trend can also be observed on the validation loss curves. Although the performance of the model trained with SGD is significantly worse than Adam, we can still see similar phenomena as Adam.

1b47fefd-e6cf-4450-b56b-b40faff9cdc9.label: text_chunk
1b47fefd-e6cf-4450-b56b-b40faff9cdc9.text: The BLEU score is just above zero in 15 epochs without using the warm-up stage.Second, we can see that the optimization process is sensitive to the value of T warmup , which means T warmup is an important hyper-parameter in training the Post-LN Transformer.

c1eee24d-b4dd-4ec7-bcb0-33a76991dd5a.label: text_chunk
c1eee24d-b4dd-4ec7-bcb0-33a76991dd5a.text: For example, when setting T warmup = 500 , the learned models with Adam achieve only 31.16 and 2.77 in term of BLEU score for lr max = 5 e − 4 and 1 e − 3 respectively.Such a warm-up stage has several disadvantages. First, its configuration significantly affects the final performance. The practitioners need a careful hyper-parameter tuning, which is computationally expensive for large-scale NLP tasks.

138e398e-49f0-489f-a8e0-8eb0cbc06b3c.label: text_chunk
138e398e-49f0-489f-a8e0-8eb0cbc06b3c.text:
Second, the warm-up stage could slow down the op- timization. Standard optimization algorithms usually start
On Layer Normalization in the Transformer Architecture
with a large learning rate for fast convergence. However, when using the warm-up stage, the learning rate has to gradually increase from zero, which may make the training inefficient. Liu et al.

1540fc70-7028-4e6a-823e-96cc07e30350.label: text_chunk
1540fc70-7028-4e6a-823e-96cc07e30350.text:
( 2019a ) suggests that the warm-up stage plays a role in reducing the undesirably significant variance in Adam in the early stage of model training. How- ever, according to our results, the warm-up stage also helps the training of SGD. This suggests that the benefit of the warm-up stage may be not for a particular optimizer.

3.3.

499af95d-a608-4fac-b5ee-74ebc082c862.label: text_chunk
499af95d-a608-4fac-b5ee-74ebc082c862.text:
Understanding the Transformer at initialization

We can see that the Post-LN Transformer cannot be trained with a large learning rate from scratch. This motivates us to investigate what happens at the model initialization. We first introduce the parameter initialization setting for our theoretical analysis and then present our theoretical findings.

Notations We denote L ( · ) as the loss function of one po- sition, ˜ L ( · ) as the loss function of the whole sequence,

20d5542f-be0d-4d34-b8ca-a3777e4d319c.label: text_chunk

Given a matrix of size n in × n out , the Xavier initialization sets the value of each element by independently sampling from Gaussian distribu- tion N (0 , 2 n in + n out ) .

The bias vectors are usually initialized as zero vectors.

 The scale γ in the layer normalization is set to one.For theoretical analysis, we study a simpler setting. First, we focus on single-head attention instead of the multi- head variant and for all layers, we set the shape of W Q,l , W K,l , W V,l , W 1 ,l , W 2 ,l to be d × d .

08c2f9dc-66ce-4122-bc91-3226544390b1.text:

 Third, we assume the input vectors are also sampled from the same Gaussian distribution.

452bd989-d7ba-4d75-a7c4-946004dd764e.text: Dif- ferent from the Post-LN Transformer that puts the layer nor- malization between the residual blocks, the Pre-LN Trans- former puts the layer normalization inside the residual con- nection and places it before all other non-linear transforma- tions. Additionally, the Pre-LN Transformer uses a final layer normalization right before the prediction.

2597b665-1683-4936-87bc-da22db05bed1.text: From Theorem 1, we can see that for the Post-LN Trans- former, the scale of the gradients to the last FFN layer is of order O ( d √ ln d ) which is independent of L . For the Pre-LN Transformer, the scale of the gradients is much smaller. We first study the forward propagation of the Post- LN Transformer and the Pre-LN Transformer.

c00e7a88-8f49-44ff-900a-dd0745094524.label: text_chunk
c00e7a88-8f49-44ff-900a-dd0745094524.text: Lemma 1 will be served as a basic tool to prove the main theorem and other lemmas.Lemma 1. If X ∈ R d is a Gaussian vector, X ∼ N (0 , σ 2 I d ) , then E ( k ReLU ( X ) k 2 2 ) = 1 2 σ 2 d .

At initialization, for the Post-LN Transformer, E ( k x post, 5 l,i k 2 2 ) = 3 2 d for all l > 0 and i .

Expectations are taken over the input and the randomness of initialization.Lemma 2 studies the expected norm of the hidden states in both Post-LN/Pre-LN Transformer.

677e7074-67af-4b48-98c8-f6da8b2bcf1d.label: text_chunk
677e7074-67af-4b48-98c8-f6da8b2bcf1d.text: It is obviously that in the Post-LN Transformer, the norm of x post l,i is √ d and thus we study the norm of x post, 5 l,i instead. As we can see from Lemma 2, the scale of the hidden states in the Post-LN Transformer keeps to be the same in expectation while the scale of the hidden states in the Pre-LN Transformer grows linearly along with the depth.

15f337d0-502b-4aee-9e7c-4db486787897.label: text_chunk
15f337d0-502b-4aee-9e7c-4db486787897.text: The next lemma shows that the scale of the hidden states highly relates to the scale of the gradient in the architectures using layer normalization.Lemma 3. For x ∈ R d , we have k J LN ( x ) k 2 = O ( √ d k x k 2 ) in which J LN ( x ) = ∂ LN ( x ) ∂x .

064b9d8b-6a4a-4712-acfa-3e7549d4292e.label: text_chunk
064b9d8b-6a4a-4712-acfa-3e7549d4292e.text: The proof of Lemma 1, Lemma 2, Lemma 3, and Theorem 1 can be found in the supplementary material. The main idea is that the layer normalization will normalize the gra- dients. In the Post-LN Transformer, the scale of the inputs to the layer normalization is independent of L , and thus the gradients of parameters in the last layer are independent of L .

4dc21b3a-aa89-4fdf-9f4d-a95860ff16c1.label: text_chunk
4dc21b3a-aa89-4fdf-9f4d-a95860ff16c1.text: While in the Pre-LN Transformer, the scale of the input to the final layer normalization is linear in L , and thus the gradients of all parameters will be normalized by √ L .Extended theory to other layers/parameters We have provided a formal proof on the gradients of the last FFN sub- layer as above. In order to fully understand the optimization, we also make some preliminary analysis for other layers and other parameters.

86efdc0a-ad1c-4424-9a84-0b397098ee3b.label: text_chunk
86efdc0a-ad1c-4424-9a84-0b397098ee3b.text:
Our main result is that the gradient norm in the Post-LN Transformer is large for the parameters near the output and will be likely to decay as the layer index l decreases. On the contrary, the gradient norm in the Pre- Transformer will be likely to stay the same for any layer l . All the preliminary theoretical results are provided in the
supplementary material.

3.4.

6bace8fa-299a-4ddb-ab27-0ddb644f392f.label: text_chunk
6bace8fa-299a-4ddb-ab27-0ddb644f392f.text:
Empirical veriﬁcation of the theory and discussion

As our theory is derived based on several simplifications of the problem, we conduct experiments to study whether our theoretical insights are consistent with what we observe in real scenarios. The general model and training configuration exactly follow Section 3.2.

1acc9f68-a151-4ec3-8016-3fc2a62d7549.label: text_chunk
1acc9f68-a151-4ec3-8016-3fc2a62d7549.text:
The experiments are repeated ten times using different random seeds.On the concentration property Given an initialized model, we record the hidden states in the Post-LN/Pre-LN Transformer across batches and find that the norm of the hidden states satisfies the property ((0.

1,0.125)-bounded).On Theorem 1 Theorem 1 suggests that for any sizes of the Post-LN Transformer, the scale of the gradient norm in the last FFN sub-layer remains the same.

2ead5643-11bf-4756-8961-816055d333ba.label: text_chunk
2ead5643-11bf-4756-8961-816055d333ba.text:
On the contrary, that of the Pre-LN Transformer decreases as the size of the model grows.

 We calculate and record the gradient norm in the last FFN sub-layer in 6-6/8-8/10-10/12-12/14-14 Post- LN/Pre-LN Transformer models at initialization. The results are plotted in Figure 3(c) and 3(d) .

710257b3-8dce-4718-87cf-c729f1455bf3.text:
The x-axis is the size of the model, and the y-axis is the value of the gradient norm of W 2 in the final FFN sub-layer.

 The figures show when the number of layers grows, the gradient norm remains in the Post-LN Transformer (around 1.6) and decreases in the Pre-LN Transformer.

df1269ce-630c-49f8-8842-9687e6497089.label: text_chunk
df1269ce-630c-49f8-8842-9687e6497089.text:
This observation is consistent with our theory.On the extended theory We calculate the gradient norm of each paramter matrix in 6-6 Post-LN/Pre-LN Transformer. We record the gradient for each parameter for different mini- batches.

 For elements in a parameter matrix, we calculate their expected gradients and use the Frobenius norm of those values as the scale of the expected gradient of the matrix.

c27f5696-20db-4555-8699-d6f92432ae18.label: text_chunk
c27f5696-20db-4555-8699-d6f92432ae18.text:
Figure 3(a) and 3(b) shows those statistics for FFN sub-layers. The x-axis indexes different Transformer layers. It can be seen from the figure, the scale of the expected gradients grows along with the layer index for the Post-LN Transformer.

 On the contrary, the scale almost keeps the same for different layers in the Pre-LN Transformer.

811f8518-69ac-4d92-bd16-c543b50ca220.label: text_chunk
811f8518-69ac-4d92-bd16-c543b50ca220.text: These observations are consistent with our theoretical findings.The critical warm-up stage for Post-LN Transformer Given the analysis above, we hypothesize that the gradi- ent scale is one of the reasons that the Post-LN Transformer needs a careful learning rate scheduling. Since the gradients are large for some layers, using a large learning rate without warm-up may make the training unstable.

e513d262-f909-49bb-9b1e-b47b8289ff31.label: text_chunk
e513d262-f909-49bb-9b1e-b47b8289ff31.text:
To verify this argument, first, we study the gradient statistics for the Post-LN Transformer after the warm-up stage with Adam. It can be seen from Figure 3(a) and 3(b) that the scale

1928ed4d-6b7c-422c-81ca-1ff385a53a3c.label: text_chunk
1928ed4d-6b7c-422c-81ca-1ff385a53a3c.text: of the gradients are very small, and the model can be trained with large learning rates. Second, we conduct an experiment to train the Post-LN Transformer from scratch using a fixed small learning rate, i.e., 1 e − 4 , to verify whether using small- step updates mitigates the issue. The details are provided in the supplementary material.

155dd05a-4c1b-4ac0-aaf0-70c112c9c03f.label: text_chunk
155dd05a-4c1b-4ac0-aaf0-70c112c9c03f.text:
In general, using a very small and fixed learning rate can mitigate the problem and optimize the Post-LN Transformer to a certain extent but the convergence is significantly slower. Both experiments above are supportive to our claim.

4. Experiments

We find in the previous section that the gradients at initializa- tion for Pre-LN Transformer are well-behaved.

de5dcc36-c24a-467d-959b-8dc868b61156.label: text_chunk
de5dcc36-c24a-467d-959b-8dc868b61156.text:
Given this observation, we deduce that the learning rate warm-up stage can be safely removed when training Pre-LN Transformer. In this section, we empirically verify it on two main tasks in NLP, machine translation and unsupervised pre-training.

4.1.

93bdcc4b-18fb-48f0-8032-40b11926367d.label: text_chunk
93bdcc4b-18fb-48f0-8032-40b11926367d.text:
Experiment Settings

Machine Translation We conduct our experiments on two widely used tasks: the IWSLT14 German-to-English (De-En) task and the WMT14 English-to-German (En-De) task. For the IWSLT14 De-En task, we use the same model configuration as in Section 3. For the WMT14 En-De task, we use the Transformer base setting. More details can be found in the supplementary material.

9fb4160d-9ecf-4289-b76a-6d1b6bfb129e.label: text_chunk
9fb4160d-9ecf-4289-b76a-6d1b6bfb129e.text: For training the Pre-LN Transformer, we remove the learn- ing rate warm-up stage. On the IWSLT14 De-En task, we set the initial learning rate to be 5 e − 4 and decay the learning rate at the 8-th epoch by 0.1. On the WMT14 En-De task, we run two experiments in which the initial learning rates are set to be 7 e − 4 / 1 .

48b809ac-aef6-43c8-b46b-b939922ca308.label: text_chunk
48b809ac-aef6-43c8-b46b-b939922ca308.text: 5 e − 3 respectively. Both learning rates are decayed at the 6-th epoch followed by the inverse square root learning rate scheduler.We train the Post-LN Transformer using the learning rate warm-up stage as the baseline. In both IWSLT14 De-En task and WMT14 En-De task, we set the number of the warm-up stage to be 4000 following Vaswani et al.

( 2017 ) and then use the inverse square root learning rate scheduler. For all
experiments above, we use the Adam optimizer and set the hyper-parameter β to be (0 . 9 , 0 . 98) . We set lr max as same as the initial learning rates of the Pre-LN Transformer in each corresponding experiment. Since Liu et al.

We use linear learning rate decay suggested by Liu et al. ( 2019a ) and keep all other hyper-parameters to be the same as in other experiments.Unsupervised Pre-training (BERT) We follow ( Devlin et al.

We follow the suggestions from ( Devlin et al. , 2018 ) to crawl and collect BookCorpus on our own. The concatenation of two datasets contains roughly 3.

c4c7a87e-8c80-41c8-8e4a-751e8c50b128.label: text_chunk
c4c7a87e-8c80-41c8-8e4a-751e8c50b128.text: 4B words in total, which is compara- ble with the data corpus used in ( Devlin et al. , 2018 ). We randomly split documents into one training set and one vali- dation set. The training-validation ratio for pre-training is 199:1.We use base model configuration in our experiments.

d6d94447-e163-4307-9121-094017edf951.label: text_chunk
d6d94447-e163-4307-9121-094017edf951.text: Simi- lar to the translation task, we train the Pre-LN BERT without the warm-up stage and compare it with the Post-LN BERT. We follow the same hyper-parameter configuration in Devlin et al. ( 2018 ) to train the Post-LN BERT using 10k warm- up steps with lr max = 1 e − 4 .

eccdc88f-3dfe-48a2-abd6-fbb3029bed8a.label: text_chunk
eccdc88f-3dfe-48a2-abd6-fbb3029bed8a.text:
For the Pre-LN BERT, we use linear learning rate decay starting from 3 e − 4 without the warm-up stage. We have tried to use a larger learning rate (such as 3 e − 4 ) for the Post-LN BERT but found the optimization diverged.

4.2. Experiment Results

Machine Translation We record the model checkpoints for every epoch during training and calculate the validation loss and BLEU score.

8552ee45-99d5-48b6-961d-b14d48ae85f6.label: text_chunk
8552ee45-99d5-48b6-961d-b14d48ae85f6.text:
The performance of the models at different checkpoints are plotted in Figure 4(a) - 4(d) .First, as we can see from the figure, the learning rate warm- up stage is not critical anymore for training the Pre-LN Transformer and the performance of the learned model is competitive. For example, on the IWSLT14 De-En task, the

964807d2-f2d8-4ac7-81a2-679d6571bae3.text:
Performances of the models on unsupervised pre-training (BERT) and downstream tasks
BLEU score and validation loss of the Pre-LN Transformer can achieve around 34 and 4, which are comparable with the performance of the Post-LN Transformer.Second, the Pre-LN Transformer converges faster than the Post-LN Transformer using the same lr max .

22fa4a4a-431c-4fcd-8a52-1243dae0fcb6.label: text_chunk
22fa4a4a-431c-4fcd-8a52-1243dae0fcb6.text: On the IWSLT14 De-En task, the 9-th checkpoint of the Pre-LN Transformer achieves nearly the same performance (vali- dation loss/BLEU score) as 15-th checkpoint of the Post- LN Transformer. Similar observations can be found in the WMT14 En-De task.Third, compared with RAdam, we find that the change of the position of layer normalization “dominates” the change of the optimizer.

833e71ad-fcf8-489a-9876-2cb85c9245c0.label: text_chunk
833e71ad-fcf8-489a-9876-2cb85c9245c0.text: According to our experiments on the IWSLT14 De-En task, we can see that although RAdam trains the Post-LN Transformer well without the warm-up stage, it has little difference with Adam when training the Pre-LN Transformer.Unsupervised Pre-training (BERT) We record valida- tion loss of the model checkpoints and plot them in Figure 5(a) .

34d8c7a3-93bf-4671-8f7c-9fd462351e73.label: text_chunk
34d8c7a3-93bf-4671-8f7c-9fd462351e73.text:
Similar to the machine translation tasks, the learning rate warm-up stage can be removed for the Pre-LN model.

 The Pre-LN model can be trained faster. For example, the Post-LN model achieves 1.69 validation loss at 500k updates while the Pre-LN model achieves similar validation loss at 700k updates, which suggests there is a 40% speed-up rate.

187d4c95-9532-45c2-8210-4e901f4aad4d.label: text_chunk
187d4c95-9532-45c2-8210-4e901f4aad4d.text:
Note that T warmup (10k) is far less than the acceleration
(200k) which suggests the Pre-LN Transformer is easier to optimize using larger learning rates.

 We also evaluate different model checkpoints on the downstream task MRPC and RTE (more details can be found in the supplementary material). The experiments results are plotted in Figure 5(b) and 5(c) . We can see that the Pre-LN model also converges faster on the downstream tasks.

631daea3-ac91-4a1e-9304-4b0a1e86ab9a.label: text_chunk
631daea3-ac91-4a1e-9304-4b0a1e86ab9a.text:
As a summary, all the experiments on different tasks show that training the Pre-LN Transformer does not rely on the learning rate warm-up stage and can be trained much faster than the Post-LN Transformer.

5. Conclusion and Future Work

In this paper, we study why the learning rate warm-up stage is important in training the Transformer and show that the location of layer normalization matters.

ac2d91b9-38d2-49fe-a158-fe70a6f7586e.label: text_chunk
ac2d91b9-38d2-49fe-a158-fe70a6f7586e.text:
We show that in the original Transformer, which locates the layer normalization outside the residual blocks, the expected gradients of the parameters near the output layer are large at initialization. This leads to an unstable training when using a large learning rate.

 We further show that the Transformer which locates the layer normalization inside the residual blocks, can be trained without the warm-up stage and converges much faster.

fab5ae3c-7e36-4ff3-86f2-ada241a4f54d.label: text_chunk
fab5ae3c-7e36-4ff3-86f2-ada241a4f54d.text:
In the future, we will investigate other strategies of positioning the layer normalization and understand the optimization of Transformer from a theoretical perspective.On Layer Normalization in the Transformer Architecture

In arXiv preprint arXiv:1506.06724 , 2015.On Layer Normalization in the Transformer Architecture

A. Experimental Settings

A.1. Machine Translation

Experiment on Section 3 The training/validation/test sets of the IWSLT14 German-to-English (De-En) task con- tain about 153K/7K/7K sentence pairs, respectively.

f2ee61bf-4e7c-407b-88e5-be76597ff7a9.label: text_chunk
f2ee61bf-4e7c-407b-88e5-be76597ff7a9.text: We use a vocabulary of 10K tokens based on a joint source and target byte pair encoding (BPE) ( Sennrich et al. , 2015 ). All of our experiments use a Transformer architecture with a 6-layer encoder and 6-layer decoder.

64bafa8d-51cb-444d-bbde-99acb0143dbc.label: text_chunk
64bafa8d-51cb-444d-bbde-99acb0143dbc.text: The size of embedding is set to 512, the size of hidden nodes in attention sub-layer and position-wise feed-forward network sub-layer are set to 512 and 1024, and the number of heads is set to 4. Label smoothed cross entropy is used as the objective function by setting = 0 . 1 ( Szegedy et al. , 2016 ), and we apply dropout with a ratio 0.1.

4b631356-5c27-4e97-a47b-2f23b906784e.label: text_chunk
4b631356-5c27-4e97-a47b-2f23b906784e.text: The batch size is set to be 4096 tokens. When we decode translation results from the model during inference, we set beam size as 5 and the length penalty as 1.2.Experiment on Section 4 The configuration of IWLST14 De-En task is the same as in Section 3 1 . For the WMT14 En-De task, we replicate the setup of ( Vaswani et al.

fefacd0e-d152-4c5a-90cf-a9f115a450dd.label: text_chunk
fefacd0e-d152-4c5a-90cf-a9f115a450dd.text: , 2017 ), which consists of about 4.5M training parallel sentence pairs, and uses a 37K vocabulary based on a joint source and target BPE. Newstest2013 is used as the validation set, and Newstest2014 is used as the test set. One of the basic configurations of the Transformer architecture is the base setting, which consists of a 6-layer encoder and 6-layer decoder.

b169cf5c-56cb-4295-8033-6bd86cd7ede1.label: text_chunk
b169cf5c-56cb-4295-8033-6bd86cd7ede1.text:
The size of the hidden nodes and embeddings are set to 512. The number of heads is 8. Label smoothed cross entropy is used as the objective function by setting = 0 . 1 . The batch size is set to be 8192 tokens per GPU on 16 NVIDIA Tesla P40 GPUs.

A.2. Unsupervised Pretraining

We follow Devlin et al.

7b6198ce-ae16-4cfc-97db-e1ad498b998c.label: text_chunk
7b6198ce-ae16-4cfc-97db-e1ad498b998c.text: ( 2018 ) to use English Wikipedia corpus and BookCorpus for the pre-training. As the dataset BookCorpus ( Zhu et al. , 2015 ) is no longer freely distributed. We follow the suggestions from Devlin et al. ( 2018 ) to crawl and collect BookCorpus 2 on our own. The concatenation of two datasets includes roughly 3.

b3b323f6-a659-4e80-a1b8-06e1d0f5b21f.label: text_chunk
b3b323f6-a659-4e80-a1b8-06e1d0f5b21f.text:
4B words in total, which is comparable with the data corpus used in Devlin et al. ( 2018 ). We first segment documents into sentences with Spacy 3 ; Then, we normalize, lower-case, and tokenize texts
using Moses ( Koehn et al. , 2007 ) and apply BPE( Sennrich et al. , 2016 ). We randomly split documents into one training set and one validation set.

16a32a0b-f74a-4bb6-864d-96fda6bad72d.label: text_chunk
16a32a0b-f74a-4bb6-864d-96fda6bad72d.text:
The training-validation ratio for pre-training is 199:1. All experiments are conducted on 32 NVIDIA Tesla P40 GPUs.The base model in Devlin et al. ( 2018 ) consists of 12 Trans- former layers. The size of hidden nodes and embeddings are set to 768, and the number of heads is set to 12.

A.3.

08a99ac4-8371-4cc2-b82a-26922d71cd85.label: text_chunk
08a99ac4-8371-4cc2-b82a-26922d71cd85.text:
GLUE Dataset

MRPC The Microsoft Research Paraphrase Corpus ( Dolan & Brockett , 2005 ) is a corpus of sentence pairs auto- matically extracted from online news sources, with human annotations for whether the sentences in the pair are seman- tically equivalent, and the task is to predict the equivalence. The performance is evaluated by the accuracy.

37b123a3-62f1-4f36-8498-9e90c494cddb.label: text_chunk
37b123a3-62f1-4f36-8498-9e90c494cddb.text: RTE The Recognizing Textual Entailment (RTE) datasets come from a series of annual textual entailment challenges ( Bentivogli et al. , 2009 ). The task is to predict whether sen- tences in a sentence pair are entailment. The performance is evaluated by the accuracy.Fine-tuning on GLUE tasks We use the validation set for evaluation. To fine-tune the models, following Devlin et al.

face4567-fe3f-4e8f-8bea-1e5405fac472.label: text_chunk
face4567-fe3f-4e8f-8bea-1e5405fac472.text: ( 2018 ); Liu et al. ( 2019b ), we search the optimization hyper- parameters in a search space including different batch sizes (16/32), learning rates ( 1 e − 5 - 1 e − 4 ) and number of epochs (3-8).

274ea3b6-b4c1-4959-b156-d5e39a8a6fe6.label: text_chunk
274ea3b6-b4c1-4959-b156-d5e39a8a6fe6.text:
We find that the validation accuracy are sensitive to random seeds, so we repeat fine-tuning on each task for 6 times using different random seeds and compute the 95% confidence interval of validation accuracy.

G. Examples of (ϵ, δ)-bounded random variables

3a2e7b5e-05b3-4ed9-85a7-6c9e6e025a30.text:
H. Small learning rate experiment

Theoretically, we find that the gradients of the parameters near the output layers are very large for the Post-LN Trans- former and suggest using large learning rates to those pa- rameters makes the training unstable.

c4eabd19-2ac0-4742-b24b-051608c3a60c.label: text_chunk
c4eabd19-2ac0-4742-b24b-051608c3a60c.text:
To verify whether using small-step updates mitigates the issue, we use a very small but fixed learning rate and check whether it can op- timize the Post-LN Transformer (without the learning rate warm-up step) to a certain extent.

 In detail, we use a fixed learning rate of 1 e − 4 at the beginning of the optimization, which is much smaller than the lr max = 1 e − 3 in the pa- per.

b7e72dee-1581-4767-99e8-bb0e85744a57.label: text_chunk
b7e72dee-1581-4767-99e8-bb0e85744a57.text:
Please note that as the learning rates during training are small, the training converges slowly, and this setting is not very practical in real large-scale tasks. We plot the validation curve together with other baseline approaches in Figure 6.

 We can see from the figure, the validation loss (pink curve) is around 4.3 in 27 epochs. This loss is much lower than that of the Post-LN Transformer trained using a large learning rate (blue curve).

de8ad64f-80cd-4974-adef-3b6a9fb73a4a.label: text_chunk
de8ad64f-80cd-4974-adef-3b6a9fb73a4a.text:
But it is still worse than the SOTA performance (green curve).On Layer Normalization in the Transformer Architecture

The relationships outlined in the knowledge graph highlight various entities and their interconnections within the context of machine learning and neural network architectures. Here is a summary of the key relationships:

1. **BERT and XLNet**: Both BERT and XLNet utilize **learning rate warm-up** as a training technique to enhance stability and performance during model training.

2. **Post-LN Transformer Layer**: This layer employs **Layer Normalization** after the residual connection, indicating its architectural design choice to stabilize training.

3. **MultiHead Attention**: The **MultiHead Attention** mechanism processes the entire input sequence \([x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n]\) to compute attention scores for specific positions, facilitating effective information retrieval.

4. **Feed-Forward Network (FFN)**: The FFN incorporates the **ReLU activation function** to introduce non-linearity, which is crucial for learning complex patterns in data.

5. **Xavier Initialization**: This technique involves sampling from a **Gaussian Distribution** to initialize parameter matrices in neural networks, promoting effective training.

6. **Post-LN/Pre-LN Transformer**: This architecture utilizes a **softmax layer** to generate a probability distribution over its vocabulary, essential for tasks like sequence prediction.

7. **Vocabulary**: The vocabulary is constructed using **Byte Pair Encoding**, a method for tokenizing text data efficiently.

8. **Normal Distribution (N) and Chernoff Bound**: The **Chernoff bound** is applied to estimate probabilities associated with the normal distribution \(N(0, 1)\), demonstrating its utility in statistical analysis.

Overall, these relationships illustrate the integration of various techniques and components that contribute to the effectiveness and efficiency of modern neural network architectures.

The Pre-LN Transformer is a significant architecture in the field of natural language processing (NLP) that influences various aspects of model training and evaluation. 

1. **Warm-up Stage Elimination**: The design of the Pre-LN Transformer leads to the elimination of the warm-up stage in training, showcasing how architectural choices can impact training methodologies.

2. **Model Type**: This architecture is applicable in training models like BERT, which is specifically tailored for NLP tasks, highlighting its versatility and relevance in modern language processing frameworks.

3. **Application in NLP**: The Pre-LN Transformer is explicitly designed for NLP applications, reinforcing its importance in this domain.

4. **Performance Evaluation**: It is evaluated on the IWSLT14 DE-EN dataset, a benchmark for machine translation, to assess its effectiveness in translating between German and English.

5. **Performance Metrics**: The quality of translations produced by the Pre-LN Transformer is quantified using the BLEU metric, a standard measure for evaluating the performance of machine translation systems.

In summary, the Pre-LN Transformer is a pivotal model in NLP that not only streamlines training processes by removing unnecessary stages but also demonstrates its efficacy through rigorous evaluation on established datasets and metrics.

The ReClor dataset is a specialized resource derived from standardized graduate admission examinations and modified from the work of the Law School Admission Council. It is specifically designed to create exams that assess logical reasoning abilities, targeting graduate students preparing for admission to graduate schools. 

ReClor is comparable in size to the DREAM dataset and shares similarities with ImageNet, although it focuses on reasoning capabilities rather than visual recognition. Additionally, ReClor plays a significant role in advancing research in natural language processing (NLP) and can be applied contextually to evaluate language models in understanding agricultural practices, such as slash-and-burn agriculture. 

Overall, ReClor serves as a critical tool for enhancing logical reasoning assessment and contributes to the broader field of NLP research.

2. **C. Szegedy, W. Liu, P. Sermanet, D. Anguelov, D. Erhan, V. Vanhoucke, A. Rabinovich** - All co-authors of the influential research paper "Going deeper with convolutions," which has significantly impacted convolutional neural network architectures.

4. **G. Mesnil, N. Dehak, P. Kenny** - Contributed to articles published in the "IEEE/ACM Transactions on Audio, Speech and Language Processing," enhancing the understanding of audio processing.

5. **S. Bengio** - Authored a chapter in "The Handbook of Multimodal-Multisensor Interfaces," contributing to multimodal interface research.

6. **B. Plummer, E. Park** - Involved in projects "Flickr30k entities" and "Visual Madlibs," respectively, both of which focus on image and language processing.

7. **H. Lee, T. Xu** - Co-authored significant works on "Generative adversarial text to image synthesis" and "AttnGAN," contributing to advancements in generative models.

8. **A. Ephrat, I. Mosseri** - Co-authors of a paper in "ACM Transactions on Graphics," contributing to graphics and visual computing.

9. **J. Pennington** - Co-authored the GloVe model paper, which is crucial for word representation in natural language processing.

10. **R. Kiros** - Co-authored a paper on "Unifying visual-semantic embeddings with multimodal neural language models," bridging visual and textual data.

11. **I. Goodfellow, T. Salimans, J. Pouget-Abadie** - All contributed to the development of Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs), with papers on improved training techniques and foundational GAN concepts.

12. **Z. Yu, J. Yu** - Co-authors of a paper in the "IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems," contributing to neural network research.

13. **A. Deshpande, Y. Guo, C. Gan, M. Mirza, E. Denton** - Each contributed to various significant papers and projects, including image captioning, dataset development (MS-Celeb-1M), and generative models.

14. **S. Nam, Y. Kim** - Co-authored a paper on "Text-adaptive generative adversarial networks," focusing on interactive image editing.

15. **P. Cascante-Bonilla, Y. Chen** - Involved in research on dialog-based platforms and interactive image editing.

16. **U. Jain** - Co-authored a paper on generating diverse questions using variational autoencoders, contributing to creativity in AI.

1. **Neural Module Network**: This entity generates a **Deep Model** specifically designed to produce answer predictions tailored to the format of target questions. It emphasizes the modular approach to building models that can adapt to different question types.

2. **Neural Network Language Model**: This model is responsible for generating **Text Embeddings** by estimating the probability of text sequences. This process is crucial for deriving embeddings that capture the semantic meaning of text, facilitating various natural language processing tasks.

3. **RNN Question Encoder**: This encoder generates **Layout Policies** by transforming input questions into structured formats. These policies are essential for guiding the processing of information, ensuring that the model can effectively interpret and respond to queries.

4. **Recurrent MAC Cell**: This component generates hidden states using an **ANN Model** that incorporates attention and gating mechanisms. This relationship underscores the importance of recurrent architectures in managing and processing sequential data.

Overall, these relationships illustrate a cohesive framework where different neural network components collaborate to generate models and embeddings that enhance the understanding and processing of questions and text.
